questions.q: What did you learn in Academic Vocabulary And Concept?
questions.hint: Read the notes first, then answer.

notes.text: Addition is one of the most basic arithmetic operations and is used to combine two or more digits/numbers together. Addends are the digits/numbers being added, and the result of the final answer we get after the process is called the sum. The symbol of addition is "+\".
Addition Statement is a mathematical expression that demonstrates two or more digits/numbers added together and their sum.
4+37 is an addition statement.
Subtraction is an arithmetic operation and is used to remove or take away objects or numbers from a given collection of objects or from another number. The symbol of addition is "-".
The number being subtracted is the subtraend, while the number it is subtracted from is the minuend. The result is the difference.
Word/Story Problem serves as a narrative tool that presents a real-world situation requiring a mathematical solution.
Times Table is a list of multiples of a number. Times table of any number can be obtained by adding the same number in every next step.
Cardinal numbers are numbers that are used for counting real objects or counting things. They are also known as "counting numbers" or "cardinals."
Ordinal numbers are numbers that are used to represent the rank or position of an object or a person. They are also referred to as positioning or ranking numbers.

Measurement: is the process of finding how much, how long, how heavy, or how big something is by comparing it with a standard unit. It helps us describe and compare objects using numbers. Measurement includes concepts such as length, weight, capacity, time, and temperature.
Data Handling: is the process of collecting, organizing, and understanding information in charts or tables so we can read, compare, and use it easily.
Pattern is a regular arrangement or sequence of repeating objects, lines, shapes, colours or numbers related to each other in a specific rule.

Shape: describes the form or outline of an object. It shows the object's outer boundary such as its sides, corners, or curved edges. Shape tells us about how an object looks and it is different from its color, size, texture, or material.
Plane Shape: are two-dimensional shapes or flat shapes that can be drawn on paper. They have only length and width and have no thickness. Their edges can be straight or curved.
Examples: Triangle, Square, Rectangle, Circle.
Solid Shape: are three-dimensional shapes. It has measurable length, breadth/width, and height. A solid figure takes up space and the volume.
Examples: Cube, Cuboid, Sphere.
Times Table is a list of multiples of a number. Times table of any number can be obtained by adding the same number in every next step.
Comparison is a process or method to determine whether a number or quantity is smaller, greater, or equal to another number or quantity according to their values or amount.
E.g. The symbols used for comparing numbers or quantities are “☐”, which means “greater than”; “☐”, which means “less than”; and “=”, which means “equal to”.
Ascending Order the process of arranging the numbers in increasing order, i.e., from smallest value to largest value. The lowest value is written first and the highest value is written at the last.
The word Ascending means “Going Up”
Descending Order the process of arranging the numbers in decreasing order, i.e., from largest value to smallest value. The highest value is written first and the lowest value is written at the last.
The word Descending means “Going Down”

notes.text: Number Sense - I
Introduction to Digit, Numbers and Numerals
Counting Numbers (Till 100)
Missing Numbers
Skip counting (2, 5, 10s)
Times Table (2, 5, 10)
Before, Between, and After
Comparison (More than/Less than, Bigger and Smaller)
Even and Odd Numbers
Number Names (Till 99)
Place Values (Tens and Ones)
- High Order Cognitive Abilities (Critical Thinking, Problem Solving, Decision Making, Creativity, Empathy, Respect, Collaboration, etc.)
Academic Vocab
Real life application / Tell me Why?
Imagine a World without Numbers!
A clock without Numerals: How would you know if it's your school time or lunch time or bed time?
A calendar without Numbers: Birthday? Independence Day? Republic Day? Everyday becomes, Some-day, day!
A Snake and Ladder board without numbers: how will you know whether you climbed, slipped, won, or lost?
While helping someone find your home, we often say, “Mine is the house before that shop,” or “Just skip two houses and you'll reach!”
No digits on the dial-pad! How will you dial your friend?
Writing the amount on the bank cheque in words would become impossible without knowing place values. Isn't it?
##### Tip for Parent:

Do you know when we say 2 one-za two, it actually means 2 ones are Two (1+1=2), and 2 two-za four means 2 twos are Four (2+2=4). While your child recites tables, listen carefully and guide them to understand the meaning behind the words.
The concept of comparison (More than/Less than) can be taught to the children with the help of fruits/vegetables available in the vegetable basket.

notes.text: ##### High Order Cognitive Abilities
##### Abilities
Problem Solving
Decision Making
You are planning a surprise birthday party for the child of your househelp, and you have to design everything from the very beginning.

You start by writing down how many friends you want to invite. Each time the number grows big, you bundle ten friends into one special "team" and see how many full teams and how many single friends you have left. Then, you give each friend a fun number name.
Then you plan how to decorate the room by choosing your own balloon pattern—maybe grouping them in 2s, 5s, or 10s—You can draw it in your notebook too.
You also choose how big the birthday cake should be and think about whether the number of slices will be enough for all the friends you invited.
Next, you pack the return gifts. You arrange the gifts in straight rows of ten and see if any gifts are left over at the end.
Finally, you organise the cups, plates, and chocolates. Instead of counting them one by one, you stack them in tens and then place the extra ones beside them, just like a part supply shopkeeper.
Are you ready to plan a wonderful surprise party for your friend?
Share with us how you felt organising a party for your househelp child.
On a Sunday morning, you saw your mother organise your cupboard. What is the first thing you should do? You should help your mother organise your cupboard. Right?

Next, you decide where each group should go. You keep big and heavy items on the lower shelves and light and small items on the upper shelves. You arrange your school books in ascending order and your clothes in the descending order of size from the biggest to the smallest. Try noticing the place value of the number of books too.

Now it is time to fix your socks. You pair them in groups of two and find out if the total number of socks is even or odd. If one sock is left without a pair, you know the number is odd. If all socks are paired, the number is even.

Then you move to your toys. You arrange them on the shelf and make number bonds of 10. For example, if you keep 6 toys in one box, how many more are needed to make 10? You add some more toys and see: , , . You are using addition without even realising it!
Finally, you make up a small story about how you spent Sunday morning arranging your cupboard with the help of your mother!
Do you feel proud of yourself as you used sorting, comparing, counting, addition, number bonds, place value and ordering to make your cupboard beautiful.

chapters.title: Real Life Application Tell Me Why

notes.text: Imagine a World without Numbers!
In a lift, if floor numbers are not in order, how will you know whether the lift is going up or down?
Imagine a chocolate broken into pieces and sharing it with your friends. You may give away a few pieces to your friend, but you know... It was one whole chocolate.
When you add chairs for guests, sweets for friends, candles on a cake - silently at the back of your mind you are doing addition.

Without comparison, will it be possible for you to know that the World's talles statue of Lord Ram was unveiled at Gokarn Partagali Math in the year 2025? Would you be able to know who is the fastest runner, the smallest animal?
From helping your mother in the kitchen to sharing snacks with your friends, life full of stories. And these stories turn into memories that stay with you for lifetime.
Ask your child to count chairs, plates or sweets when guests come so they see how addition happens in real life.
When you read news or watch TV together point cut dates, ages, distances are score's so your child notices numbers every where.

questions.q: What did you learn in Figure Me Out?
questions.hint: Read the notes first, then answer.

notes.text: Observe the given table and answer the following questions.
Q1. The teacher asks Ron to find out the favourite snack of his friends. Ron collects the data and records it in the table shown below. Observe the given data and answer the questions:
A. How many friends like ice-cream?
B. How many friends like burger?
c. How many friends like pizza?
D. Which snack is liked by 5 of his friends?
E. Which two snacks add up to 7?
F. How many friends like brownie and burger?
Q2. Sam sells many items in his shop. The table below shows the number of items sold different days of the week. Observe the given data and answer the questions.
Monday 🔔🔔🔔🔔🔔🔔🔔
Tuesday 🔔🔔🔔🔔🔔🔔
Wednesday 🏠🏠🏠
Thursday 🔔🔔🔔🔔🔔
Friday 🔔🔔🔔🔔🔔
Saturday 🏠🏠🏠🏠
A. How many clocks were sold on Friday?
B. Which item was sold on Saturday?
C. On which day maximum number of items were sold?
D. On which day the least number of items were sold?
